title: IRC BRIDGE CODES - Overview
content: # IRC:SP-18 - Manual for Structural Evaluation of Concrete Bridges

title: IRC BRIDGE CODES - Section 1: Shear Cracking on Piers and Girders
content:
Section 1: Shear Cracking on Piers and Girders

- **Critical Diagnostics**: Diagonal cracks inclined at 45 degrees near the support (pier junctions) are shear cracks.
- Unlike flexural cracks, shear cracks are brittle and dangerous.
- **Urgency Thresholds**:
  - Crack width < 1.0 mm: Monitor closely at 3-month intervals.
  - Crack width 1.0 - 3.0 mm: Schedule pressure grouting with low-viscosity epoxy resin.
  - Crack width > 3.0 mm: Critical structural danger. Impose speed and axle-load restrictions immediately. Restructure bridge girder loads.
  - Crack width > 5.0 mm: Imminent local shear failure. Close the bridge span to heavy freight immediately.

title: IRC BRIDGE CODES - Section 2: Concrete Spalling on Deck Girders
content:
Section 2: Concrete Spalling on Deck Girders

- Spalling on the underside of deck slabs or girders weakens the tensile flange.
- If section loss of tensile reinforcement exceeds 10%, structural integrity is compromised.
- **Section Loss and Rehabilitation (Section 6)**:
  - Section Loss < 10%: Clean rebar, apply rust inhibitors, restore profile using polymer-modified cementitious mortar.
  - Section Loss 10% - 25%: Install carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) wraps or laminates to restore tensile capacity.
  - Section Loss > 25%: Requires welding additional steel rebars or installing external post-tensioning tendons to restore structural load rating.

title: IRC BRIDGE CODES - Section 3: Bridge Foundation Settlement
content:
Section 3: Bridge Foundation Settlement

- Tilting, differential settlement, or cracking at the base of piers indicates foundation wash-out, soil scour, or bearing failure.
- Scour inspection must be performed post-monsoon.
- Remediation: Concrete underpinning or micro-piling around the pier cap foundation.

title: IS456 CONCRETE - Overview
content: # IS 456:2000 - Plain and Reinforced Concrete Code of Practice

title: IS456 CONCRETE - Section 1: Permissible Crack Widths
content:
Section 1: Permissible Crack Widths

According to Clause 35.3.2, structural cracks in concrete structures affect durability and appearance. The limits for surface crack widths under serviceability limit state are:
- **Mild Exposure**: Surface crack width should not exceed **0.3 mm** in reinforced concrete members.
- **Moderate Exposure**: Surface crack width should not exceed **0.2 mm** (e.g., structures exposed to rain or damp basements).
- **Severe Exposure**: Surface crack width should not exceed **0.1 mm** (e.g., structures exposed to marine environments or aggressive chemicals).
- **Critical Structural Danger**: Any crack exceeding **1.5 mm** indicates shear distress or structural overloading. Immediate pressure epoxy injection is required.

title: IS456 CONCRETE - Section 2: Concrete Spalling and Reinforcement Exposure
content:
Section 2: Concrete Spalling and Reinforcement Exposure

Clause 26.4 states that concrete cover protects reinforcement from corrosion and fire.
- **Nominal Cover Requirements**:
  - Slab: 20 mm
  - Beam: 25 mm
  - Column: 40 mm
  - Foundation: 50 mm
- **Spalling Action Limits**:
  - When concrete spalls and reinforcement is exposed, the carbonation front has reached the steel.
  - Exposure of reinforcement bars leads to steel section loss. Active scaling rust reduces the tensile capacity.
  - **Clause 11.3 Repair Standard**: Expose the corroded steel completely, clean using wire brushes or sandblasting, apply a zinc-rich anti-corrosion primer, and patch with polymer-modified mortar or micro-concrete.

title: IS456 CONCRETE - Section 3: Concrete Leaching and Carbonation
content:
Section 3: Concrete Leaching and Carbonation

- **Calcium Leaching**: White efflorescence or carbonate deposits on concrete surfaces (especially in tunnels or dams) indicate that water is leaching calcium hydroxide.
- Leaching increases concrete porosity, dropping compressive strength and paving the way for carbonation.
- Remediation: Drill injection ports and pump polyurethane chemical grout to stop water ingress.

title: NDMA LANDSLIDE RETAINING - Overview
content: # NDMA Guidelines: Landslide Risk Mitigation & Retaining Structures

title: NDMA LANDSLIDE RETAINING - Section 1: Retaining Wall Structural Inspections
content:
Section 1: Retaining Wall Structural Inspections

Retaining walls (masonry, reinforced concrete, or gabion) are critical for protecting highway roads, bridges, and mountain structures.
- **Defect Indicators**:
  - **Tilted Wall**: Indicates earth pressure exceeding design capacity or soil liquefaction behind the wall.
  - **Weep Hole Blockage**: Blocked weep holes trap water behind the wall, creating hydrostatic pressure. This is the #1 cause of retaining wall collapse.
  - **Horizontal Cracking**: Indicates bending stress. Cracks > 2.0 mm on concrete retaining walls require immediate pressure relief.
- **Remediation**:
  - Clear clogged weep holes. Drill new weep holes at 1.5m grid intervals.
  - Install ground anchors (soil nailing) to anchor the retaining wall back into stable rock.

title: NDMA LANDSLIDE RETAINING - Section 2: Slope Stability and Soil Erosion
content:
Section 2: Slope Stability and Soil Erosion

- Drainage structures, catch drains, and concrete-lined chutes must be free of debris.
- Soil slopes near highway bridge abutments require geotextile membrane covers or shotcrete application to prevent erosion under heavy monsoons.
- If cracks form in the soil slope crest, it indicates tension cracking and imminent landslide failure. Fill cracks with compacted clay immediately to prevent water ingress.
